title: Bacterial Staining and Microscopy
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"csrc0","text":"Overview and Goals","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":18}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"aajb3","text":"Now that you have streaked your unknown bacteria for isolation, you will take steps to identify certain properties of the organisms via a variation of the gram stain. Broadly, bacteria can be categorized into Gram-positive and Gram-negative groups based on the presence or absence of an outer cell membrane and a thick peptidoglycan layer (cell wall). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d5qb","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ase0","text":"Learning Objectives\nAfter completing this lab, you will gain the following lab skills:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":19}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2qcrv","text":"Lab safety and proper personal protective equipment (PPE)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ev78n","text":"Use of Gram staining/ Gram staining alternative to identify the gram status of a bacterial isolate","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5qcmt","text":"Use of a fluorescent microscope for the detection of red/green fluorescence ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"25d8k","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"16gq5","text":"Before starting","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":15}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bc0hv","text":"Review the complete protocol before beginning. Several of the steps in this procedure are time and/or temperature-sensitive, so you must know what to expect and how to manage your time. Work as a team! There are also several types of tubes with specific names that need to be used for specific protocol steps. They are shown in the illustration below to help you keep track of these materials.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"deh17","text":"Each group will need: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1c6gk","text":"Slides and cover slips","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fv369","text":"Sterile PBS","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8j4ve","text":"Bacterial culture/plate for isolates","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cr09m","text":"BUG agar plate with bacteria (incubated overnight at 33°C from your TSA plates)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"47uhe","text":"Invitrogen LIVE BacLight kit","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":28}],"data":{}},{"key":"5se65","text":"MycoLight‱ rapid fluorescence bacterial gram stain kit","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":0,"length":54}],"data":{}},{"key":"313jg","text":"Micropipette tips for p200, p20, and p10","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bupt9","text":"Wipes","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"666rj","text":"Tip disposal container","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"4886D75891B64C31A40302C7FF2F86DE","url":"https://www.thermofisher.com/order/catalog/product/L7012"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"457B4821BA99459593C2B1BD15D86DF6","url":"https://www.aatbio.com/products/mycolight-rapid-fluorescence-bacterial-gram-stain-kit"}}}}
steps: Take bacteria (1 or 2 colonies) from a plate using a sterile swab or loop.
Combine equal volumes of Component A and Component B in a microfuge tube and mix thoroughly. You were given 5 µL of each component.
Add 3 µL of the dye mixture per mL of bacterial suspension.
Mix thoroughly and incubate at room temperature in the dark for 15 minutes. 00:15:00, in the dark at room temperature
Trap 20 µL of each stained bacterial suspension between a slide and a 24x50 mm rectangular coverslip.

Observe in a fluorescence microscope equipped with any of the filter sets listed in Table 1. Live Gram-negative organisms should fluoresce green, and Gram-positive bacteria should fluoresce red. %s %s
Cleanup Instructions. All liquids containing bacteria must be poured into a beaker for bleach decontamination (can be found on the blue bench paper by this sink labeled "liquid").
All bacterial growth plates can be thrown away in the biohazard trash
Return all reusable materials to our storage drawers.
Wipe down all surfaces with Conflikt and then ethanol.
Remove PPE.
Wash hands.

Save images and analyze results. Critical Thinking Questions: Microscopy Protocol 1 What advantages and disadvantages can you think of when using fluorescent dyes instead of crystal violet (the traditional gram stain dye)? Why do you think it is important that SYTO9 and Hexidium Iodide not have significantly overlapping emission spectra? An iodine and then an alcohol wash are the final steps of a gram stain. Based on your understanding of lipid membranes and cell walls, what do you think the purpose of these reagents is? (Hint: iodine creates a high molecular weight complex with crystal violet) Why might it be important to use a gram stain or acceptable alternative to identify the gram status of a bacterial isolate in a medical setting?

Review the protocol. Please review the complete protocol before beginning. Several of the steps in this procedure are time—and/or temperature-sensitive, so it’s important that you know what to expect and how to manage your time. We will thaw all the kit components at room temperature before use and spin down briefly. We have created single-use aliquots to avoid freeze-thaw cycles and stored them at -20°C after preparation. We prepared the MycoLight‱ Red stock solution previously by adding 100 µL of ddH2O into one vial of MycoLight‱ Red (Component B) and mixing well. Instructors have also prepared the MycoLight‱ dye working solution by mixing equal volume of MycoLight‱ Green (Component A) and MycoLight‱ Red stock solution in a tube.

Grow isolates overnight by picking a colony from an agar plate and inoculating 2.5 mL of Tryptic Soy Broth (TSB). Grow at 30 °C for 16-18 hours. Dilute in fresh medium and grow for 2-3 hours. The instructors will perform this step.

We will prepare bacteria samples with concentrations around 10 7 cells/ml. Grow bacteria into late log phase in an appropriate medium. Note: According to the manufacturer, for Escherichia coli culture, OD600 = 1.0 equals 8 x 10 8 cells/ml. The instructors will help with this step.
Remove medium by centrifugation at 10.000 x g, °C, 10 minutes and re-suspend the pellet in ddH2O, adjust bacteria concentration to ~ 10 7 cells/ml.
Add 2 µL MycoLight™ dye working solution to 100 µL of the bacterial suspension.
Mix well and incubate in the dark for 00:15:00 at room temperature. You can store the solution in a 1.5 ml tube in your drawer.
Remove the working solution by centrifugation at 10000 x g, °C, 10 minutes
Resuspend the bacteria pellet in 100 µL volume of ddH2O.
Add 10 µL of stained cells to a clean slide and cover with a coverslip. Provide the rest of the stained cells to your instructor for imaging. Table 2. MycoLight Bacterial Staining %s %s

Monitor the fluorescence of bacteria with a fluorescent microscope or the Agilent LionHeart. Critical Thinking Questions: Microscopy Protocol 2 What do your results suggest about your isolate? What shape does your organism have? Do your results support the previous staining procedure we used? Why or why not?
Mix bacteria into 1 mL PBS (bacterial suspension).
creator: Carlos Carlos Goller
